content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


November 24, 2004


Gregory P. Priamos, City Attorney
City of Riverside
Office of the City Attorney
3900 Main Street
Riverside, CA 92522

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-247

Dear Mr. Priamos:

This letter is in response to your request for clarification of advice on behalf of
Councilmember Dom Betro relating to the Priamos Advice Letter, Our File No. A-04-
191, which pertains to the gift and campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

You have asked for clarification regarding whether Mr. Betro is required to report
payments made to the Family Service Association, a nonprofit exempt from taxation
under Internal Revenue Service section 501(c)(3), which he may behest in connection
with his employment.

• We previously advised that the payments would not be considered gifts to the council
member. Thus, the payments would not be reportable on his statement of economic
interests, nor would they be subject to any gift limit imposed by the Act.

• Under the definition of "contribution," section 82015, payments made to a nonprofit
at the behest of the council member are considered reportable contributions where it
is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment is made for purposes
related to his or her candidacy for elective office. As we advised in the prior letter,
under the limited facts set forth in the Priamos Advice Letter, supra, the payments
would be made for purposes unrelated to Mr. Betro's candidacy and therefore would
not be contributions. (Section 82015.) Since they are not contributions to the council

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04- 247
Page No. 2

member, he would not be required to report the payments on his campaign disclosure
forms.

• With respect to the $5,000 reporting under section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii), again, under
the limited facts set forth in the Priamos Advice Letter, supra, the city council
member would not have to report the payments in question. However, we cautioned
that if the facts are not as set forth in our prior advice letter but are still made
principally for charitable purposes, the payments may be reportable at the $5,000
threshold under section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii). A determination of whether any
particular payment is ultimately reportable under the Act is a question of fact. The
Commission is not the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
(Section 82015.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

JWW:jg

sections.question: Is Councilmember Dom Betro required to report payments made to the Family Service Association, a nonprofit exempt from taxation under Internal Revenue Service section 501(c)(3), which he may behest in connection with his employment?
sections.conclusion: The payments would not be considered gifts to the council member and thus would not be reportable on his statement of economic interests, nor would they be subject to any gift limit. Under the limited facts presented, the payments would not be contributions since they are made for purposes unrelated to Mr. Betro's candidacy for elective office, and therefore would not be required to be reported on his campaign disclosure forms. However, if the facts differ but payments are still made principally for charitable purposes, they may be reportable at the $5,000 threshold under section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii).
sections.question_synthetic: The question seeks clarification on whether a city councilmember must report charitable payments made at his behest to a nonprofit organization under the Political Reform Act's gift and campaign contribution provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the payments in question are not reportable as gifts or campaign contributions under the Political Reform Act, provided the facts remain as stated. However, the FPPC cautions that determinations of reportability are fact-dependent and reserves that different circumstances might result in $5,000 threshold reporting requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a follow-up advice letter (A-04-247) clarifying prior advice in file A-04-191. Contains clear question and conclusion sections embedded in narrative form. Minor OCR artifacts present (e.g., 'Fa.rn Por-rucal' for 'Fair Political'). Confidence reduced slightly due to formatting without explicit section headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is Councilmember Dom Betro required to report payments made to the Family Service Association, a nonprofit exempt from taxation under Internal Revenue Service section 501(c)(3), which he may behest in connection with his employment?

CONCLUSION: The payments would not be considered gifts to the council member and thus would not be reportable on his statement of economic interests, nor would they be subject to any gift limit. Under the limited facts presented, the payments would not be contributions since they are made for purposes unrelated to Mr. Betro's candidacy for elective office, and therefore would not be required to be reported on his campaign disclosure forms. However, if the facts differ but payments are still made principally for charitable purposes, they may be reportable at the $5,000 threshold under section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii).

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request for clarification regarding Councilmember Dom Betro's reporting obligations for charitable payments made to a nonprofit organization, clarifying that under the stated facts, such payments are neither reportable gifts nor campaign contributions.
